credit-card.notes: Georgia applies a 6-year limitation to written contracts. However, credit cards may be treated as open accounts subject to a 4-year limitation (O.C.G.A. § 9-3-25). Georgia courts have split on this — the 4-year period is more commonly applied to credit cards. The Georgia Fair Business Practices Act supplements FDCPA protections.
credit-card.citation: O.C.G.A. § 9-3-25 (open account, 4 years); O.C.G.A. § 9-3-24 (written contract, 6 years)

medical.notes: Medical debts in Georgia based on written contracts are subject to the 6-year limitation. Oral agreements or implied contracts may be subject to a 4-year period.

auto-loan.notes: Auto loan deficiency balances in Georgia follow the 6-year written contract limitation from the date of the deficiency.

personal-loan.notes: Personal loans in Georgia are subject to the 6-year limitation for written contracts.

student-loan.notes: Private student loans in Georgia are subject to the 6-year written contract limitation. Federal loans are exempt from state SOL for government collection.

mortgage-deficiency.notes: Georgia allows a 7-year limitation on sealed instruments and mortgages. Deficiency claims after non-judicial foreclosure must be brought within 30 days of the sale.

utility-bill.notes: Utility bill debts in Georgia are typically treated as open accounts subject to the 4-year limitation.

regulatoryBody: Georgia Department of Banking and Finance & Georgia Attorney General
complaints: File a complaint with GA DBF at dbf.georgia.gov or the GA AG at law.georgia.gov. Georgia's Fair Business Practices Act (O.C.G.A. § 10-1-390 et seq.) provides additional consumer protections.
